selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: Look, I'm going to be straight with you—I don't use social media myself, and honestly, that's probably saved me a lot of headaches. But I've got four kids and I see what's happening out there, and it worries me. When you're running your own carpentry business like I do, you learn pretty quick that you need to verify things before you trust them. Same principle applies to fake content online. The way I see it, managing fake content comes down to being methodical and not rushing to believe everything you see. First, you've got to develop what I call a "builder's skepticism." Just like I wouldn't use materials without checking the quality first, you shouldn't share information without checking the source. Look at who's posting it, where it's coming from, and whether it's corroborated by multiple reliable sources. My kids have learned from watching me that credibility matters. Second, take your time. I work with precision in my trade because mistakes cost money and create problems down the line. The same applies here—don't immediately share or react to something that triggers your emotions. Pause, breathe, and investigate. I've noticed people tend to share things that make them angry or excited without thinking, and that's exactly how fake content spreads. Third, teach the people around you, especially younger folks, to do the same. I talk to my children about being critical thinkers, about understanding that not everything on a screen is real. It's like teaching them to spot poor craftsmanship—once you know what to look for, you see the flaws. Finally, consider limiting your time on these platforms if they're causing you stress. I've chosen not to participate, and I don't regret it one bit. My attention goes to my work, my kids, my hobbies, and my community. That's real life. Fake content thrives on engagement and outrage, so the less you feed into that cycle, the better off you'll be.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 40-year-old self-employed carpenter with high conscientiousness (69) and extraversion (73), but low openness (23). He has no social media presence and values practical, hands-on approaches. His high conscientiousness suggests he appreciates systematic, methodical solutions. His moderate agreeableness (49) and high neuroticism (69) indicate he's somewhat skeptical and emotionally reactive, making the topic personally relevant despite his non-participation. His vocational background and creative nature inform his preference for concrete, practical advice. The tone should reflect working-class pragmatism, skepticism toward digital platforms, and a focus on verification and methodical thinking. Examples should draw from his carpentry work, family responsibilities, and direct experience observing social media's effects on others rather than personal usage.
response_parsed.tone: Pragmatic, direct, slightly skeptical, protective, experienced-based, plain-spoken with working-class sensibility, concerned but measured in approach
